Rainfall totals across the area ranged from 1 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey by Putnam County EMA along with radar data indicate a brief EF-0 tornado touched down south of Baxter. One home on Palmer Lane suffered garage door damage near the start of the path and a mobile home lost part of its roof on Thomas Twin Oak Road at Palmer Lane. A barn was damaged on Moss Road north of Alton Boyd Road. Several trees were snapped and uprooted along the intermittent damage path. Additional minor wind damage including snapped tree branches and minor exterior damage to a greenhouse occurred further to the northeast along Ditty Road and Cowan Road, but the damage was too minor to be certain if this was part of the tornado path. This is the first known tornado to occur in Putnam County in the month of August.

The system brought numerous showers and thunderstorms to Middle Tennessee, some of which became severe and produced several reports of damaging winds along with two weak tornadoes. Rainfall totals across the area ranged from 1 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary frontal boundary stalled across the central portions of Middle Tennessee from Saturday, September 12, 2020 into Sunday, September 13, 2020. Persistent southwest flow aloft brought copious amounts of Gulf of Mexico moisture northward and interacted with this boundary, causing a roughly 7 hour period of nearly continuous heavy rain and thunderstorms. Rainfall totals reached 5 to over 8 in some locations, resulting in major flash flooding and river flooding along a narrow corridor across Lawrence, Lewis, Maury, Williamson, Davidson, Rutherford, Wilson, Sumner, and Macon Counties. Numerous water rescues were conducted and many homes and businesses were flooded, with some of the worst flooding occurring in the Mill Creek basin across northeast Williamson County and southeast Davidson County. In fact, Mill Creek at Nolensville reached 19.53 feet, which is the second highest crest ever at that location - only surpassed by the record of 22.53 feet in the May 2010 flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters covered the road at Rock Springs Road and Rogana Road.

Numerous roads were also flooded and closed including Baronswood Drive, Kidd Road, Nolensville Road, Sunset Road, York Road, Old Clovercroft Road, Summerlyn Drive, Oldham Drive, Concord Road, Rocky Springs Road, Rocky Ford Road, Stonebrook Blvd., Dortch Lane, McFarlin Road, Fly Road, and Brittain Lane. The Brittain Lane bridge over Mill Creek was damaged by the flooding and indefinitely closed. Five water rescues were conducted in the area with many vehicles submerged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding caused several shipping containers at the Bridgestone Plant to float to Bridgestone Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary frontal boundary stalled across the central portions of Middle Tennessee from Saturday, September 12, 2020 into Sunday, September 13, 2020. Persistent southwest flow aloft brought copious amounts of Gulf of Mexico moisture northward and interacted with this boundary, causing a roughly 7 hour period of nearly continuous heavy rain and thunderstorms. Rainfall totals reached 5 to over 8 in some locations, resulting in major flash flooding and river flooding along a narrow corridor across Lawrence, Lewis, Maury, Williamson, Davidson, Rutherford, Wilson, Sumner, and Macon Counties. Numerous water rescues were conducted and many homes and businesses were flooded, with some of the worst flooding occurring in the Mill Creek basin across northeast Williamson County and southeast Davidson County. In fact, Mill Creek at Nolensville reached 19.53 feet, which is the second highest crest ever at that location - only surpassed by the record of 22.53 feet in the May 2010 flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: I-24 was closed at Bell Road due to water over the interstate around Blue Hole Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system tracked across the Missouri Bootheel and northwest Tennessee and into the TN River River by mid-afternoon on September 1st. In the wake of the departing mesoscale convective system, an isolated thunderstorm developed over the Missouri Bootheel and moved into Lake County, Tennessee. A couple of weak tornadoes occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flooding along Turkey Creek, south of Camden.  A foot of water was reported over Gobbler Drive near Highway 192.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front that moved across Middle Tennessee from late on November 29 into November 30 produced widespread rain across the area. As an area of low pressure moved eastward along the front, temperatures dropped during the day allowing the rain to change over to snow before ending during the evening. Locations along and east of I-24 saw anywhere from a dusting to over 4 inches of snow, with the highest amounts falling on the northern Cumberland Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to one half inch of snow fell across Marshall County. CoCoRaHS station Lewisburg 6.2 SSE measured 0.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked along a warm front stretched just north of the Mid-South. Meanwhile, a potent upper-level disturbance tracked into the region increasing wind fields and triggering thunderstorms along the warm front and south of the front, in the warm sector, during the late evening hours of March 2, 2020.  A couple of supercells tracked across the Missouri Bootheel and northwest Tennessee during the late evening hours. One supercell spawned three tornadoes across Gibson, Carroll, and Benton counties. A few severe storms also developed along a trailing cold front across eastern Arkansas and West Tennessee during the early morning hours of March 3, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage to homes and mobile homes was observed from just west of Highway 45E to Highway 105 near the Gibson-Carroll County line. Numerous trees were also damaged along this path. The most significant damage was observed along Gann Road between Goosefoot and Belews Chapel Road. Peak winds were estimated at 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms affected Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours on October 23, a few of which became strong to severe. One report of large hail was received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system developed along an outflow boundary and tracked south into eastern Arkansas, southwest Tennessee, and north Mississippi.  Very unstable air along and south of the boundary fed these storms resulting in numerous severe weather reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines knocked down across the Memphis area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Cyclone Zeta tracked across the southeastern United States, producing locally heavy rainfall across the area, and windy conditions across the southern Appalachians.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind tower at Clingman's Dome measured a wind gust of 50 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the Mid-South during the late afternoon and early evening hours on October 23rd. The front interacted with some marginal instability and a severe storm developed over Carroll County in West Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on June 30. A few storms became severe across southwest Middle Tennessee with several reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down south of Loretto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level disturbance moving through northwest flow aloft interacted with an outflow boundary across West Tennessee and North Mississippi to produce a few severe storms. A couple of tornadoes, damaging winds, and small hail occurred with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down on Leach Road and traveled southeast crossing Purdy Road and Poplar Springs Road near the Thousand Lakes Community. The tornado continued southeast toward Highway 114 where two homes suffered roof damage. Trees were uprooted and snapped along the path, but overall damage was intermittent along the track. Peak winds were estimated at 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Depression Cristobal moved through the region on June 9, 2020. This system brought some heavy rain and flash flooding to parts of the Mid-South including north Mississippi and southwest Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fleming Road flooded between Shelby Drive and Collierville Road. Shelby Drive flooded between Houston Levee Road and Quinn Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon produced pockets of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were reported down on Clover Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system developed along an outflow boundary and tracked south into eastern Arkansas, southwest Tennessee, and north Mississippi.  Very unstable air along and south of the boundary fed these storms resulting in numerous severe weather reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line winds damaged the roof of a gas station at the intersection of Poplar Avenue and Collierville-Arlington Road.

A large area of thunderstorms and heavy rain developed north of a stalled boundary across the Mid-South and produced several areas of flooding in Tipton County, Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred near Main Street and L and N Roads in Mason.  A few homes were evacuated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of thunderstorms and heavy rain developed north of a stalled boundary across the Mid-South and produced several areas of flooding in Tipton County, Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding along Highway 59 at Mason-Malone Road, Highway 14 intersection, and in the Cannon Grove area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level disturbance interacted with a moist and unstable airmass to produce numerous thunderstorms across West Tennessee during the afternoon hours on August 31st. A lightning strike resulted in an injury in Henderson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike occurred near the NIDEC plant in northeast Lexington.  One injury resulted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought high winds and heavy snow to parts of East Tennessee. The storm began with rain and mountain wave high winds during the early morning hours of 12/24. In the afternoon, cold air rapidly swept into the area, changing rain to snow. Moderate to heavy snow fell for about 3-4 hours that evening, resulting in several inches of accumulation. Parts of the central and northern Valley reported between 4 and 7 inches of snow, with up to 12 inches reported in the Smoky Mountains. Scattered to numerous snow showers continued into Christmas Day, with only light additional accumulations.

Light snow fell across eastern Middle Tennessee much of the day on Christmas Eve into the early morning hours on Christmas Day. Snowfall totals ranged a dusting along the I-65 corridor up to two inches on the northern Cumberland Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to two inches of snow fell across Fentress County. CoCoRaHS station Jamestown 3.1 SE measured 1.5 inches of snow, and CoCoRaHS station Clarkrange 6.0 NE measured 1.5 inches of snow.

A northwest flow snowfall event produced heavy snow in portions of the East Tennessee mountains, mainly for elevations above 2500 feet. Reported snow amounts were mainly between 4 and 7 inches, with 11 inches reported at Mt. LeConte. The snowfall began on November 30 and continued into December 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mt. LeConte recorded 11 inches of snow, with 7 inches reported on English Mountain. The snowfall began on November 30th and continued into December 1.
